questions.question_text: An Alabama broker pays a $500 referral fee to an unlicensed friend who recommended a buyer. What violation occurred?
questions.explanation: Alabama law prohibits paying any commission, fee, or compensation to unlicensed persons for real estate brokerage activities. All compensation for referrals or services must flow to properly licensed individuals through the qualifying broker.

questions.question_text: A salesperson receives a bonus directly from a grateful seller, bypassing the qualifying broker. What is wrong with this arrangement?
questions.correct_answer: All compensation must flow through the broker
questions.explanation: In Alabama, all compensation for real estate services must flow through the qualifying broker. A salesperson cannot receive any form of compensation directly from a client, seller, or other party. The broker distributes all compensation to affiliated licensees.

questions.question_text: Which scenario violates Alabama's rules on compensation to unlicensed persons?
questions.correct_answer: A broker splitting a fee with an unlicensed assistant
distractors: A broker paying a licensed out-of-state principal broker under a co-brokerage agreement
A broker compensating a licensed associate broker
A broker paying a referral to a licensed broker
questions.explanation: Splitting fees or commissions with unlicensed persons is a violation under Alabama law. Paying a licensed out-of-state principal broker under a co-brokerage agreement is permitted -- Act 2025-380 caps these at three agreements per out-of-state broker per calendar year and $50 million in annual volume, with the Alabama broker supervising. Compensating licensed associates and paying referral fees to licensed brokers are likewise permissible.

questions.question_text: An unlicensed office manager accepts a share of commission for helping close a transaction. Who is in violation?
questions.correct_answer: Both the manager and the broker
distractors: Neither if the amount is under $500
questions.explanation: Alabama law prohibits both the paying and receiving of commissions involving unlicensed persons. The broker violates the law by paying the commission, and the unlicensed person violates it by receiving compensation for real estate brokerage activity. There is no dollar threshold exception.

questions.question_text: Through whom must all real estate compensation flow in Alabama?
questions.explanation: Alabama law requires that all compensation for real estate brokerage services flow through the qualifying broker. Salespersons and associate brokers cannot receive compensation directly from clients, other brokers, or any third party. The qualifying broker is responsible for distributing all earned compensation.

questions.question_text: A licensee offers a raffle for a free TV at an open house to attract potential buyers. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Conducting a lottery tied to real estate
questions.explanation: Alabama law prohibits licensees from conducting or promoting lotteries, raffles, or similar games of chance in connection with real estate transactions. Using prize drawings to attract clients at open houses falls under this prohibition.

questions.question_text: A licensee gives a buyer a $1,000 cash rebate at closing. What is the result under Alabama license law?
questions.correct_answer: It is a violation because rebates are flatly prohibited
distractors: It is permitted if the qualifying broker authorizes it
It is permitted if disclosed in writing to all parties
It is permitted as a bona fide gift to the buyer
questions.explanation: Under Section 34-27-36(b)(12), paying or receiving any rebate in a real estate transaction is a violation, so neither broker authorization nor disclosure makes it lawful. Alabama draws a sharp line here: rebates are flatly prohibited, while bona fide gifts are permitted. A cash payment tied to the closing is a rebate, not a bona fide gift.

questions.question_text: An Alabama licensee is convicted of embezzlement, a felony involving moral turpitude. What is the consequence for the license?
questions.correct_answer: Grounds for license discipline or revocation
distractors: Required to pay a $5,000 fine only
No effect if unrelated to real estate
questions.explanation: Conviction of a felony or any crime involving moral turpitude is grounds for disciplinary action against an Alabama real estate license, regardless of whether the crime is directly related to real estate. This can result in suspension or revocation of the license.

questions.question_text: Violation of any rule or order issued by AREC constitutes what?
distractors: A civil lawsuit by the Commission
questions.explanation: Violation of any rule or order of the Alabama Real Estate Commission is grounds for disciplinary action. This is a broad provision that gives AREC enforcement authority over all its regulations. Discipline may include reprimand, fine, suspension, or revocation.

questions.question_text: Which activity is specifically prohibited for Alabama real estate licensees?
questions.correct_answer: Promoting lotteries connected to real estate
questions.explanation: Alabama law specifically prohibits conducting or promoting lotteries in connection with real estate activities. Educational seminars, competitive commission rates, and social media advertising are all permissible activities for licensees.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits a client's earnest money into her personal checking account. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Commingling client funds with personal funds
questions.explanation: Commingling occurs when a licensee mixes client funds with personal or business operating funds. Alabama law requires that all client funds be deposited into a designated trust or escrow account. Commingling is a serious violation subject to disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: An Alabama broker fails to maintain records of trust account transactions for the required period. What violation has occurred?
distractors: Failure to inform of legal action
questions.explanation: Alabama law requires brokers to maintain complete and accurate records of all trust account transactions. Failure to keep appropriate records is a specific violation under Section 34-27-36 and is grounds for disciplinary action by the Commission.

questions.question_text: A broker collects rent on behalf of a property owner but fails to remit the funds. What is the primary violation?
questions.correct_answer: Failing to account for or remit money
distractors: Operating without a property management license
questions.explanation: Failing to account for or remit money belonging to others is a serious violation of Alabama license law. Brokers who collect funds on behalf of clients must properly account for and timely remit those funds. This includes rents, security deposits, and any other funds held in trust.

questions.question_text: A licensee has a civil judgment entered against her related to a failed transaction but does not report it. What duty was breached?
questions.correct_answer: Duty to report legal action to the Commission
distractors: Duty to carry errors and omissions insurance
Duty to notify the qualifying broker only
questions.explanation: Alabama licensees must report any legal action, including civil judgments, related to their real estate activities to the Commission. Failure to report is itself a separate violation subject to disciplinary action, regardless of the merits of the underlying case.

questions.question_text: Which record-keeping failure constitutes a violation of Alabama license law?
questions.correct_answer: Not maintaining escrow account transaction logs
distractors: Using digital instead of paper records
Storing records at a secondary office
Keeping records for only five years
questions.explanation: Failure to maintain proper records of escrow and trust account transactions is a violation of Alabama license law. Using digital records, maintaining records at secondary offices, and reasonable retention periods are generally acceptable practices as long as records are complete and accessible.
